description: HLLQP - LIFE 03 QZ - Whole Life and Term-100 Insurance - Section 1
examTips: Focus on whole life policy features (guaranteed premium, CSV, non-forfeiture options), T-100 characteristics, participating vs non-participating policies, dividend options, and premium payment structures.

questions.question:
Michael is 35 years old. He applied for $100,000 in whole life insurance. The insurer crunched the numbers and quoted him a premium of $162 per month. Michael will have to pay the premium every month, and it will never increase.
On a whole life policy, what is the primary purpose of the policy reserve?
options: A. To provide for the payment of future death claims.
B. To pay for the insurer's administrative expenses.
C. To provide a source of policy dividends.
D. To fund the cash surrender value of the policy.
optionFeedback: The reserve is not primarily intended to pay the insurer's administrative expenses.
Policy dividends may come from surplus on participating policies, but that is not the primary purpose of the reserve.
Cash surrender value is supported by the reserve, but the reserve's primary purpose is to ensure future death claims can be paid.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The reserve is not primarily intended to pay the insurer's administrative expenses; Policy dividends may come from surplus on participating policies, but that is not the primary purpose of the reserve.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: To provide for the payment of future death claims.

questions.question:
Caleb is 50 and did not even consider insurance until he had a son, named Turner, a few years ago. Caleb has lived a very exciting life as the lead singer in a mildly successful band. While Caleb makes a decent living, it is unlikely that he will ever strike it rich. His lifestyle has caused him to gain weight, which has contributed to high blood pressure and type 2 diabetes.
While he was still able to obtain life insurance, the policy was "rated", which resulted in a higher-than-usual premium. Caleb inquires if there is anything he can do today to ensure Turner can have some form of coverage in early adulthood regardless of his (Turner's) health.
Which of the following strategies would be appropriate?
options: A. Caleb cannot purchase life insurance in Turner's name because his son has not reached the age of majority.

B. Caleb can provide the insurer with evidence of insurability for Turner today. This will lock in Turner's right to purchase life insurance throughout his life without providing evidence of insurability.
C. Caleb could purchase a whole life insurance policy with himself as the life-insured and policyholder. When Turner is older, Caleb can change the life-insured to Turner.
D. Caleb could purchase a life insurance policy with Turner as the life-insured. When his son is older, Caleb can absolutely assign (transfer) the policy to him.
optionFeedback: A parent can purchase life insurance on a minor child; the age of majority is not a barrier.
Evidence of insurability for the insured today does not lock in future insurability for a different person (Turner).
The life-insured cannot simply be changed to a different person on an existing policy.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A parent can purchase life insurance on a minor child; the age of majority is not a barrier; Evidence of insurability for the insured today does not lock in future insurability for a different person (Turner).
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: Caleb could purchase a life insurance policy with Turner as the life-insured. When his son is older, Caleb can absolutely assign (transfer) the policy to him.

questions.question:
Harvey has a $500,000 whole life policy with over $90,000 in cash value. The annual premium of $6,000 is significantly more than what a 10-year term policy would cost at his age. Harvey has just been told by his doctor that he has cancer, and his life expectancy is less than two years. He has had second and third opinions that confirm the prognosis. Harvey is confident that he will be dead within a few years. He would like to eliminate the premium entirely but also ensure that the beneficiaries receive exactly $500,000.
Which of the following options, if any, would allow for this?
options: A. There is no way to eliminate the premium without reducing the eventual death benefit.
B. The reduced paid-up insurance non-forfeiture option
C. The extended term insurance non-forfeiture option
D. The automatic premium loan (APL) feature
optionFeedback: The extended term insurance option does eliminate premiums while keeping the full face amount intact.
Reduced paid-up insurance eliminates premiums but reduces the death benefit to a lesser permanent amount.
The APL keeps the policy in force but creates a growing loan that reduces the net death benefit below $500,000.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The extended term insurance option does eliminate premiums while keeping the full face amount intact; Reduced paid-up insurance eliminates premiums but reduces the death benefit to a lesser permanent amount.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: The extended term insurance non-forfeiture option.

questions.question:
Grace purchased a participating whole life insurance policy over 20 years ago. Each time a policy dividend was paid, it was used to buy an additional piece of paid-up insurance. Her agent has advised her that if she would like to stop paying premiums, she can surrender part of those paid-up additions (PUAs) and use the CSV along with any additional dividends to cover the entire premium. While there are no guarantees, it looks as though Grace can do this indefinitely.
What is this process referred to as?
options: A. Reduced paid-up insurance
B. Paid-up additions (PUAs) dividend option
C. Premium offset
D. Limited payment whole life insurance
optionFeedback: Reduced paid-up insurance is a non-forfeiture option that permanently reduces the face amount; it is not a premium-offset strategy.
The PUA dividend option is how the dividends were accumulated — it is not the same as using accumulated PUAs to cover future premiums.
Limited payment whole life has a set number of payment years decided at purchase, not a strategy using accumulated PUAs.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Reduced paid-up insurance is a non-forfeiture option that permanently reduces the face amount; it is not a premium-offset strategy; The PUA dividend option is how the dividends were accumulated — it is not the same as using accumulated PUAs to cover future premiums.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Premium offset.

questions.question: Kimberly purchased a T-100 policy a few months ago. She is confused about future premiums associated with the policy. What should you tell her about most T-100 policies?
options: A. They apply a level premium approach throughout the life of the policy.
B. The premium will increase each policy year.
C. The premium is guaranteed to remain level for a certain number of years, after which the premium can be adjusted upwards but not downwards.
D. The premium will remain level for a certain number of years and then will be increased or decreased depending on what transpires with mortality costs, investment returns, and mortality rates.
optionFeedback: T-100 is not a yearly renewable term policy; the premium does not increase annually.
This describes an adjustable T-100 variant, not most standard T-100 policies.
While adjustable versions exist, most T-100 policies carry a fully level and guaranteed premium.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. T-100 is not a yearly renewable term policy; the premium does not increase annually; This describes an adjustable T-100 variant, not most standard T-100 policies.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: They apply a level premium approach throughout the life of the policy.

questions.question: Whole life insurance is more expensive than term insurance. Which of the following things are guaranteed on a whole life policy?
options: A. The death benefit.
B. The cash value.
C. The premium.
D. All of the above.
optionFeedback: The death benefit is guaranteed, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
The cash value is also guaranteed on a whole life policy, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
The premium is also guaranteed on a whole life policy, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The death benefit is guaranteed, but it is not the only guaranteed feature; The cash value is also guaranteed on a whole life policy, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: All of the above.

questions.question:
Carlos is looking to purchase a permanent insurance policy and would like the plan with the lowest annual premium.
Which policy would best suit his needs?
options: A. Non-participating whole life policy
B. Universal life
C. Participating whole life policy
D. Term-100
optionFeedback: Non-participating whole life is less expensive than participating WL, but still more expensive than T-100.
Universal life premiums depend on the deposit chosen, but for basic permanent coverage, T-100 is typically cheaper.
Participating whole life is the most expensive due to built-in conservative pricing for dividend potential.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Non-participating whole life is less expensive than participating WL, but still more expensive than T-100; Universal life premiums depend on the deposit chosen, but for basic permanent coverage, T-100 is typically cheaper.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: Term-100.

questions.question:
Olivia purchased a participating policy last year. She has yet to receive a policy dividend. She is unsure of what circumstances would likely result in a dividend being paid.
Which of the following would most likely contribute to a policy dividend?
options: A. Lower than expected mortality rates.
B. Higher than expected investment returns.
C. Lower than expected administrative expenses.
D. All of the above.
optionFeedback: Lower mortality than expected can contribute to a surplus.
Higher investment returns can contribute to a surplus.
Lower expenses can contribute to a surplus.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Lower mortality than expected can contribute to a surplus; Higher investment returns can contribute to a surplus.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: All of the above.

questions.question:
Curtis has a $500,000 whole life policy with over $90,000 in cash value. His daughter, Chloe, is the named beneficiary. The monthly premium is $600. Curtis was travelling through Africa for a period of six months. During that time, his bank account went into overdraft, causing $3,600 in premium payments to be returned non-sufficient funds to the insurer. Tragically, Curtis was killed when he was trampled by a herd of elephants.
How much can his daughter expect to receive from the life insurance policy?
options: A. The policy would have lapsed after the 30-day grace period for missed premiums. A cheque would have been sent to Curtis for the cash surrender value. This payout will form part of the estate and will be distributed in accordance with his will.
B. $500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.
C. Nothing; the policy would have lapsed after a grace period (usually 30 days).
D. $500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.
optionFeedback: With $90,000+ in CSV, the APL non-forfeiture option would have automatically taken over — the policy would not have lapsed, and no CSV cheque would have been sent.
The policy would not have lapsed because the APL feature automatically uses the CSV to pay overdue premiums when the CSV is sufficient.

"$500,000 less any balance owing under an APL." is a different beneficiary designation than the one this question is testing. The better answer here is "$500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.".
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. With $90,000+ in CSV, the APL non-forfeiture option would have automatically taken over — the policy would not have lapsed, and no CSV cheque would have been sent; The policy would not have lapsed because the APL feature automatically uses the CSV to pay overdue premiums when the CSV is sufficient.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: $500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.

questions.question: After discussing the various dividend options available on a participating policy, Gabriel is leaning towards the PUA dividend option. Why is it a good idea for the agent to show her more than one dividend projection?
options: A. To illustrate the impact of different dividend scales.
B. To comply with provincial regulations.
C. To show the flexibility of the policy.
D. To demonstrate the impact of policy loans.
optionFeedback: The purpose is not simply to satisfy a provincial regulation requirement.
The core reason is to compare how different dividend scales affect outcomes, not merely to show general flexibility.
Policy loans are separate from dividend scale illustrations.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The purpose is not simply to satisfy a provincial regulation requirement; The core reason is to compare how different dividend scales affect outcomes, not merely to show general flexibility.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: To illustrate the impact of different dividend scales.
